text: In the wild wild west of AI music
generation, it seems like things are
constantly changing, especially insunoi.
And for beginners, it can feel like
trying to ride a horse hopped up on
cocoa from Mississippi to Alabama in a
hurricane during the summertime. Well,
I'm going to merk that horse and
explains AAI to you nice and easy like.
But why should you listen to me? Well,
hello. My name is Chill Panny. I've been
producing human [music] music for the
past 10 years. And I now use that
knowledge to teach you how to use Zuno
AI like a real [music] deal music
producer. So, let's start by breaking
down the various pricing tiers so you
can know which one is best for you. So,
in Sunno, they have three different
plans. The free plan, the pro plan, and
the premier plan. So, in the free plan,
you get 50 credits a day, which
basically translates to 10 songs because
each generation cost five credits. But
Sunno will create two generations per
song. So even though it says 10 songs,
it's really more like five. You also get
access to Sunno V4.5
all and their latest model is V5. So you
only get access to V5 in the pro plan
and the premier plan. You can upload up
to 1 minute of audio and you get no
add-on credit purchases and you also get
no commercial use. So in the free plan,
Sunno owns everything that you make.
Next up, we've got the pro plan, which
this is going to be useful for most
people. You get access to the latest AI
music generation models, which right now
is V5. You get 2500 credits or about 500
songs. You also get access to the
persona feature, which will come in
handy if you are trying to create an AI
artist or use your own voice. You can
also get stems with the pro plan. You
can upload up to eight minutes of audio
and you get early access to new features
and you can even purchase add-on
credits. Now, the next one, the premier
plan. This is only going to be valuable
to you if you become like a power user
like me. I'm on this plan because I like
having all those extra credits and I
like having access to Sunno Studio. But
if you're just getting started, I would
definitely recommend the free or the pro
plan. Oh, and I should have said before
also, you get commercial use rights for
the new songs that you make in the pro
plan as well as well as the premiere
plan, which basically means you can
upload to Spotify, YouTube, and all of
that. Basically, the only difference
between the pro plan and the premier
plan is that premier plan gives you
studio and gives you a [ __ ] ton of
credits. This is set to yearly.
Companies like to do that to make it
seem cheaper. So, if you go to monthly,
you see that the pro plan is $10 a month
and $30 a month for the premier plan,
which I know sounds expensive, but if
you're a power user, it's actually worth
it. And next up, we have the home tab.
This is where you can look at songs from
people that you're following. You can
look at trending songs or songs that
Sunno thinks that you will like. You can
also enter into remix contests, which I
haven't done yet for some reason. And
you have hooks, which is basically like

notifications and comments and all of
that stuff are. And if you go down to
this library, you can look at all of

are handy if you've made a lot of
samples that you want to take into a
doll or something like that. That's
mainly what I use playlist for. Or if
you just want to keep like the same type
of sound together. The workspaces is for
whenever you're creating certain songs.
And it just gives you a little bit more

you're not here to organize your

Remy lyrics model. The only difference

Remy is going to be a little more
risque. It even says that it may
generate content that some find
offensive and that really turned me on
to it. So, I usually use the Remy model
because it'll it'll just do more
interesting lyrics sometimes, but the
classic one works great as well. Below
that, you have this weirdness slider,
which basically determines how much
chaos Sunno is going to throw into your
prompt. If you keep this all the way at
zero, it's going to be safer
generations. If you go all the way to
100, which they call chaos mode, it's
basically a bunch of glitches. I'll just
create this so you can hear it. [music]
>> So, I wouldn't suggest putting weirdness
up very high unless you want some like
glitch samples that you can throw into
your doll. I actually do like to keep
this at around 60 because I enjoy a
little bit of randomness when I'm
creating songs, but it's up to you. At
default, this is set to 50. The style
influence slider basically tells Suno
how strongly it should follow your
prompt. So, if you're really confident
in the prompt that you've given it, and
you want something really specific,
turning this up high, even all the way
to 100, is going to be the best for you.
But if you'd rather Sunno stick to its
own knowledge and not rely as heavily on
your prompt, keep this on the lower end.
I usually keep this somewhere between 50
to 75% depending on the situation. And
after you've got everything filled out,
you can choose a song title for it if
you want to. If you don't want to, Sunno
will generate that for you. But let's
get a little extra customized. Let's
click manual. So now let's make an
actual song with our own lyrics, our own
style, and our own song title. The
prompting framework that I like to give
people is Gmiv, which stands for genre,
mood, instruments, and vocals. You don't
have to prompt it this way, but this has
been the simplest framework for me. So
for example, if you wanted something
like '90s rock, I would do '90s rock and
maybe grunge. I usually like to give it
a subgenre as well if I have a certain
sound in mind. So, that would be the
genre. And then for the mood, I would
say, well, actually, moody is good for
grunge. Uh, dirty, hopeless. Yeah,
moody, dirty, hopeless. That sounds
good. And for instruments, you might
have low gain, distorted guitars, a rock
drum kit, maybe some brushed drums, and
you could have some like clean guitars
also. And then for the vocal, you're
basically describing the personality of
the vocalist as well as the actual
techniques. So you could say like
baritone, male vocal, raspy voice, maybe
laidback delivery. Now for the lyrics,
you basically just type in your song.
You can add a bracket and then the song
structure title like if you want to have
an intro, verse, chorus, verse. So we'll
just say verse one.

direct what instruments you want to come

intro, you just want it to be a guitar,
just a guitar thing, no other

an instrumental break. So that would

is putting words into parenthesis. And
that's going to cause those words that
